text: This video demonstrates a laparoscopic right extended hemicolectomy with complete mesocolic excision. Our patient is a thirty three year old lady BMI twenty nine point eight with a large proliferative adenocarcinoma at the hepatic flexure. There were no metastasis in the lung or the liver.

The surgical team uses five laparoscopic ports for the procedure, with the patient positioned in the Trendelenburg position. The surgeon operates through the lower left ports while the assistant stands at the patient's head end. The omentum is reflected towards the upper abdomen, along with the small bowel loops, to begin the procedure.

The surgeon elevates the terminal ileum, revealing the right ureter. The peritoneum is incised along the root of the small bowel mesentery, with dissection proceeding from inferior to superior until the third part of the duodenum is visualized. A gauze piece is placed to mark the dissection area.

The surgeon elevates the ileocolic pedicle and incises the peritoneum below it, continuing dissection towards the root of the ileocolic pedicle and its junction with the superior mesenteric vein. Fibro-fatty tissue is included on the specimen side, exposing the junction of the ileocolic vein and the SMV. The posterior dissection meets the inferior dissection, and the gauze piece marking the dissection is removed.

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum from the mesocolon and continues the dissection medially to mobilize the pancreatic head. The transverse mesocolon is elevated, and dissection proceeds along the superior mesenteric vein towards the root of the transverse mesocolon. The peritoneum is incised at the root to identify the myelocolic vessels.

The surgeon dissects the ileocolic vessels, removing fibro fatty tissue onto the specimen side. The ileocolic vein is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by the artery. Dissection continues around the pancreatic head, collecting lymphatic and nodal tissue onto the specimen side. The absence of the right colic vein is noted.

The surgeon continues medial dissection to expose the proximal superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and the middle colic artery. The right gastroepiploic vein is visible but not part of the dissection. The middle colic artery is isolated, clipped, and divided, followed by the middle colic vein, which lies immediately posterior. This completes the pedicle division for the right extended hemicolectomy.

The surgeon visualizes the dissected superior mesenteric vein (SMV), ileocolic artery and vein, and middle colic vessels. A gauze piece is placed to mark the dissection site. The right colon is mobilized from medial to lateral until reaching the inferior border of the liver. Sharp dissection with cautery is used to take down the lateral attachments of the right colon, continuing up to the hepatic flexure for complete mobilization. The patient is repositioned to a reverse Trendelenburg position, and the surgeon switches to the head end, using the upper two left ports while the assistant is positioned towards the patient's feet.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to enter the gastrocolic omentum, accessing the lesser sac. Dissection proceeds towards the pylorus, removing omental adhesions to expose the right gastroepiploic vein. The posterior dissection is encountered, and a window is opened.

The surgeon continues the dissection, removing the ligated middle colic vein along with surrounding lymphatic tissue and nodes up to its root. The remaining attachments of the right colon are taken down using the harmonic scalpel, proceeding towards the hepatic flexure for complete mobilization. The visual field includes the dissected right colon bed, duodenum, pancreas, the SMV dissected to its left border, the ileocolic vein and artery, the middle colic artery and vein, the head of the pancreas, and the right gastroepiploic vein.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the small bowel mesentery and grasp the appendix to exteriorize the specimen. A mini-laparotomy is performed through the camera port site, and a wound protector is inserted. The specimen is exteriorized, and the small bowel is transected with a marking suture placed. The colon is then brought out, revealing a well-mobilized white colon with intact pedicles and a complete mesocolic excision without buttonholes.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the transverse colon and performs a side-to-side stapled ileocolic anastomosis. The bowel is deposited, and the incision measures less than five centimeters.
